sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 230, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 229 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 7.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have the exact positions:
- Top bar: row 0, cols 16-47 (32 pixels)
- Character (white): rows 11-25, cols 14-28 (bbox center ~18, 21)
- Red feet (character): rows 14-16 and 20-22, cols 11-13 (left side of character)
- Green rectangle: rows 29-49, cols 41-49 (bbox center ~39, 45)
- Sky blue: rows 32-46, cols 44-46 (inside green rectangle)
- Red blocks (near green): rows 35-37 and 41-43, cols 38-40 (left side of green rectangle)
So the character is at rows 11-25, cols 14-28. The green rectangle is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49.
The character needs to move DOWN (from row 25 to row 29, about 4 rows) and RIGHT (from col 28 to col 41, about 13 cols).
Total Manhattan distance: 4 + 13 = 17 moves.
But wait, the character is facing left (red feet on the left). I need to rotate it to face right or down.
Let me try pressing SPACE to rotate the character, then move RIGHT and DOWN.
Actually, let me first understand what SPACE does. Looking at the history:
- Initial state: character facing down (red feet on bottom)
- After SPACE: character facing left (red feet on left)
So SPACE rotates the character 90 degrees counter-clockwise. Let me verify:
- Initial: facing down (red feet on bottom)
- After SPACE: facing left (red feet on left)
- After SPACE again: facing up (red feet on top)
- After SPACE again: facing right (red feet on right)
So SPACE rotates the character 90 degrees counter-clockwise.
To face right, I need to press SPACE twice (from left to up to right). Or I can press SPACE once (from left to up) and then move UP, but that doesn't make sense.
Actually, let me reconsider. The character is currently facing left. To move RIGHT, I need to face right. To face right from left, I need to rotate 180 degrees, which is 2 SPACE presses.
But wait, maybe the character can move in any direction regardless of facing. Let me check the history.
Looking at the history, the character moved in various directions (UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT) regardless of facing. So the facing doesn't affect movement direction.
So I can just move RIGHT and DOWN to reach the green rectangle. Let me try that.
Actually, I realize I need to be more careful. The character is at rows 11-25, cols 14-28. The green rectangle is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49.
To reach the green rectangle, I need to move the character so that it overlaps with the green rectangle.
The character is 15 rows tall (11-25) and 15 cols wide (14-28). The green rectangle is 21 rows tall (29-49) and 9 cols wide (41-49).
To overlap, I need to move the character so that its bottom-right corner (row 25, col 28) overlaps with the green rectangle's top-left corner (row 29, col 41).
So I need to move DOWN 4 rows (25 to 29) and RIGHT 13 cols (28 to 41).
Total: 4 + 13 = 17 moves.
But wait, the character moves as a whole, not just the bottom-right corner. Let me think about this more carefully.
The character's bbox is rows 11-25, cols 14-28. When I move RIGHT, the entire character moves RIGHT by 1 col. When I move DOWN, the entire character moves DOWN by 1 row.
So after moving RIGHT 13 times, the character's bbox will be rows 11-25, cols 27-41.
After moving DOWN 4 times, the character's bbox will be rows 15-29, cols 27-41.
The green rectangle's bbox is rows 29-49, cols 41-49.
So after moving RIGHT 13 and DOWN 4, the character's bbox will be rows 15-29, cols 27-41. The green rectangle's bbox is rows 29-49, cols 41-49.
The character's bottom row (29) overlaps with the green rectangle's top row (29). The character's right col (41) overlaps with the green rectangle's left col (41).
So the character will overlap with the green rectangle at the bottom-right corner.
Let me try moving RIGHT 13 times and DOWN 4 times to reach the green rectangle.
But wait, I need to be careful about the order. If I move RIGHT first, the character will be at rows 11-25, cols 27-41. Then moving DOWN 4 times will put the character at rows 15-29, cols 27-41.
If I move DOWN first, the character will be at rows 15-29, cols 14-28. Then moving RIGHT 13 times will put the character at rows 15-29, cols 27-41.
Either order should work.
Let me try moving RIGHT 13 times and DOWN 4 times.
Actually, let me first try a small test: move RIGHT once and see what happens.
